critique.summary: The article criticizes a PC Gamer webpage for its excessive advertising and large data usage, highlighting that the page initially loads 37MB and continues to download more ads, accumulating nearly half a gigabyte in a few minutes. It also mentions the usefulness of RSS readers in avoiding such intrusive content.
mainPoints: The PC Gamer article page displays multiple popups and at least five visible ads upon loading.
The initial page load size is approximately 37MB, which is unusually large for a web article.
The website continues to download additional ads over time, reaching nearly half a gigabyte within five minutes.
RSS readers like NetNewsWire, Unread, Current, and Reeder can help users bypass such intrusive advertising and large data downloads.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the page loading multiple ads and popups are plausible and consistent with common web practices on ad-supported sites. The reported 37MB initial load size and continued downloading of ads totaling nearly half a gigabyte are unusually high but technically possible, especially if the site loads many high-resolution images, videos, or scripts. However, without direct measurement or corroboration from independent sources, these specific data size claims cannot be fully verified. The recommendation of RSS readers as a way to avoid such issues is accurate and widely recognized.
considerations: The article does not provide technical details or tools used to measure the data usage, which would strengthen the claims.
It does not discuss the potential reasons for such high data usage, such as video ads or auto-playing media.
The article does not consider user settings or ad blockers that might mitigate the problem.
No perspectives from PC Gamer or the website operators are included to explain or respond to these issues.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the excessive data usage caused by ads and autoplay videos on modern websites, exemplified by a PC Gamer article that downloads hundreds of megabytes. Commenters share personal experiences, suggest ad blocking tools, and lament the decline in web usability due to intrusive advertising and site bloat.

comments.author: MBCook
comments.text:
The title buried the lede.

> In the five minutes since I started writing this post the website has downloaded almost half a gigabyte of new ads.

I’m guessing this is due to autoplaying videos. *500 MB* in 5 minutes.

37 MB is petite compared to that.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a direct observation of the problem with concrete data, highlighting the massive data usage caused by ads and autoplay videos.

comments.author: userbinator
comments.text: To use a good point of reference that I've seen others also start using lately, an installation of Windows 95 is roughly 40MB, so in loading that page you've downloaded approximately one Windows 95 installation. Then another 10+ times with the 500MB more that came after.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a relatable analogy to contextualize the large data usage, helping readers understand the scale of the problem.

comments.author: WarOnPrivacy
comments.text:
In Firefox + Unlock Origin: Downloads 5.6MB and then stops loading.

Scrolling to the bottom of the page added 3MB of images and then stopped loading.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a counterpoint showing that with ad blockers or privacy extensions, data usage can be significantly reduced, reinforcing the value of such tools.

comments.author: johnwalkr
comments.text:
15 years ago I had a 7GB mobile data plan (in Japan). After 7GB, it was throttled to 100kbps. If I tethered my PC and there was an update available, or browsed modern sites (especially while tethered), this could easily be wiped out in a few days. After 7GB, sites like hackernews, google search/maps worked fine, and most websites loaded after a minute at most.

10 years ago I still had a 7GB mobile data plan (in Japan). After 7GB, it was throttled to 100kbps. If I tethered my PC and there was an update available, or browsed modern sites (especially while tethered), this could easily be wiped out in a minutes. After 7GB, sites like hackernews, google search/maps worked fine, although most search results failed to load.

5 years ago I still had a 7GB mobile data plan (in Japan). After 7GB, it was throttled to 1Mbps. If I tethered my PC and there was an update available, or browsed modern sites (especially while tethered), this could easily (and usually was) wiped out in a few minutes. Browsing reddit easily consumed 1GB in a day. After 7GB, sites like hackernews, google search/maps worked fine, although most search results failed to load.

I currently live in Europe, I am too old for dealing with the above shit or dealing with wifi in a town/restaurant/hotel so I pay for unlimited data throughout EU. But, it's fairly common while driving or training around that I end up on 3G. I understand 3G is degraded these days, but it should provide 300-2000 kbps. Almost nothing internet-related works at these speeds today. WhatsApp is the exception, it works eventually. I bet hackernews could load if you could somehow disable all the background things happening. I've had a few experiences where I reached a timeout for a login on Apple, google or MS services, and been locked out of my account for a few days because trying to login with low datarate means trying to login 30x in 10 minutes which must look suspicious.

Yesterday I was skiing at a resort and my phone was dying at an incredible rate, like 25% per hour. I don't know for certain but I suspect some app or website was retrying a download of something while in a dodgy service area. I'm sure it's happened that someone has been slightly injured going off into the trees at 2pm at a ski resort (or had a fall on parking lot ice, or fell down stairs in their home, or been run off the road), and not been able to call for help because some app has been loading ads and killed their phone battery.
comments.analysis: This detailed personal account adds depth to the discussion by linking excessive data usage to practical and safety concerns, enhancing the argument against heavy web bloat.

comments.author: jdangu
comments.text: To measure network load, open dev tools, uncheck "disable caches" then clear your browser cache then load the page. Screenshot indicates network cache is disabled so the stated number is inflated.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a technical critique of the measurement methodology, suggesting the reported data usage might be exaggerated due to cache settings.

comments.author: __natty__
comments.text:
It's not just "PC Gamer" but people making decisions behind as always. Three first people from their "Meet the Team" page [0]: Tim Clark — Brand Director (@timothydclark), Evan Lahti - Strategic Director (@elahti), Phil Savage — Global Editor-in-Chief (@Octaeder). Hopefully they can see this HN thread and people complains and do "something" about that.

[0] https://www.pcgamer.com/meet-the-team/
comments.analysis: This comment attempts to direct accountability to the responsible parties, encouraging action, though it does not add new technical insight.

comments.author: arkt8
comments.text:
I have no metrics but there is a lot (if not most of) sites with similar issues.

A simple site of lyrics, or newspapers that start videos automatically. Github was worse, now at least opens a bit more faster, but still very poorer than, example, codeberg. Sites are sites, most want to do fancy things more than to simply let user read its contents.

Would be nice a site that could track it to put some shame. By now, the better sites are just like HN, Wikipedia... unobstrusive and fast even without cache.
comments.analysis: This comment broadens the discussion to general web trends and praises sites that prioritize user experience, supporting the main argument.

comments.author: touwer
comments.text: Even more embarassing is that the article adds really nothing to whatever was written before about rss. Probably gobbled up by AI
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive without evidence and speculates negatively about the article's origin, reducing its credibility.

comments.author: umarcyber
comments.text: This was the exact motivation that led me to develop my own news feed for a vulnerability dashboard I'm working on. I would wait for my NVD API calls to finish by scrolling tech sites but was always inundated by ads...
comments.analysis: This comment offers a proactive solution inspired by the problem, showing constructive engagement.

comments.author: drnick1
comments.text: The modern Web is truly unusable without an aggressive DNS filter and/or uBlock.
comments.analysis: This comment reinforces the consensus that ad blocking is necessary for a usable web experience today.
